# Integration of Moreh vLLM for Tenstorrent with the MoAI Inference Framework

# **High-level objective**

* We aim to build an actual Tenstorrent cluster in China and use it to provide LLM inference services.  
* Currently, Moreh vLLM for Tenstorrent is closer to a state where performance tests and LLM benchmarks can be run on one or multiple bare-metal Galaxy servers. There is still a large gap between this and providing a real service on an actual Kubernetes cluster.  
* The goal of the MV-NPU team is to make Moreh vLLM for Tenstorrent a component of the service software stack running on a real cluster, and to bring it to a state where it can be continuously validated, maintained, supported, and managed.

# **Target system**

* The target cluster is composed of 8-node Wormhole Galaxy pods configured as 16\*16 chip torus, connected via 100 Gigabit Ethernet serving as the frontend network.  
  * A 64-node cluster consists of eight 8-node pods.  
* Originally, we hoped to connect a larger number of Galaxy servers into a single torus network. However, due to technical issues, current Galaxy servers cannot use optical cables (AOC cables) for chip-to-chip connections, which limits the physically connectable scale.  
* In other words, there is no direct chip-to-chip connection between 8-server pods; communication between pods is only possible through host-based TCP/IP or RoCE networking.  
* In addition, our software needs to consider changing the pod granularity in future, e.g., to 4-server or 12-server units.

![][image1]

# **Logical pods**

* A logical pod is the unit by which each workload – a vLLM instance – is allocated chips or servers.  
* By default, an 8-node physical pod is treated as an 8-node logical pod.  
* We can split an 8-node (physical) pod into multiple 4-node, 2-node, or 1-node (logical) pods.  
  * 4-node pod: 2\*2 nodes (16\*8 chips), with rings only in the horizontal direction  
  * 2-node pod: 1\*2 nodes (8\*8 chips), mesh topology  
  * 1-node pod: 1\*1 node (8\*4 chips), mesh topology  
* We can assume that the split is predefined, i.e., how each physical pod is divided into logical pods is determined at the system installation/configuration time. Dynamically constructing logical pods while assigning jobs is not yet within the development scope.

| All of the technical approaches described below may be incorrect, or there may be better alternatives. You are responsible for cross-checking and understanding the situation again, so that we can secure a technically correct execution plan. |
| :---- |

# **Goal 1\. Make Moreh vLLM run on Kubernetes**

* Tenstorrent chips and servers currently have no Kubernetes support. Tenstorrent has internal support, but it has not been made public externally. Since we have access to Tenstorrent’s internal repositories, referring to those may be helpful. Please ask Ihn (PM of the Advanced Technology office in Seoul) about this.  
* We will approach this in two ways.  
* First step: simply run the container in privileged mode. This allows the container to access all Wormhole chips on the server even without special Kubernetes support. We then launch it with explicit placement (i.e., labeling each server with its hostname, and specify that label when creating the pod), so that each rank position within the logical pod can be precisely controlled. This is a crude approach, but it is effective for providing quick support before investing in more complex development.  
* Second step: build a Kubernetes DRA plugin. NVIDIA and AMD already support DRA plugins. Google TPU also supports a DRA plugin, and because TPU requires topology-aware resource allocation similar to Tenstorrent chips, this was one of the motivations for Google Cloud to drive the DRA standard. The DRA standard is fairly complex, but we can refer to what these vendors have already built and implement our own DRAM plugin. Completing it properly will likely take around six months.

As execution items for the first step:

* Successfully run *Moreh vLLM for Tenstorrent* on a single Galaxy server. Verify that it works properly by calling the API. The deliverable for this item is the YAML file written to deploy *Moreh vLLM for Tenstorrent*. If necessary, *Moreh vLLM for Tenstorrent* may be modified, for example to revise the image build or initialization process. You can submit a PR and get the review from the NPU team in Seoul.  
* For future work, validate *Moreh vLLM for Tenstorrent* using *Huginn*. *Huginn* is an endpoint quality testing tool being developed by the Product team in Seoul. It is intended to validate various LLM benchmark scores and agent compatibility. If any issues are found during this process, report them to the NPU team in Seoul.  
* Successfully run *Moreh vLLM for Tenstorrent* across four Galaxy servers and run the GLM-5.1 model. My understanding is that the GLM-5.1 model is currently launched via mpirun in an environment where the four servers know each other’s hostnames and can SSH into each other. Converting this into a deployment process using four Kubernetes Pods may require some creative and tricky ideas. The deliverable for this item is the YAML file written to deploy *Moreh vLLM for Tenstorrent* across four nodes. This YAML file will include explicit node placement. If necessary, *Moreh vLLM for Tenstorrent* may likewise be modified.

# **Goal 2\. Odin integration**

* Enable *Moreh vLLM for Tenstorrent* to run by creating an Odin InferenceService, rather than by deploying it from a fully specified YAML file.  
* This should cover all logical pod configurations: 1-node, 2-node, 4-node, and 8-node logical pods.

# **Goal 3\. MoAI Performance Gateway integration**

* My understanding is that when an InferenceService is launched, a sidecar is started together with vLLM, and this sidecar allows the *MoAI Performance Gateway* to discover the new InferenceService. We need to make the same mechanism work for Tenstorrent as well, so that when an InferenceService is launched, the *MoAI Performance Gateway* can recognize it.  
* When the NPU team in Seoul adds support for PD disaggregation – where prefill and decode run on separate logical pods and communicate over the host network, i.e. 100 Gbps RoCE – make sure this runs in an integrated way with the *MoAI Performance Gateway*.  
* The *MoAI Performance Gateway* should be able to properly receive KV events from *Moreh vLLM for Tenstorrent*, so that prefix cache-aware routing can work correctly.  
* In the future, additional routing techniques will need to be incorporated as well. In particular, request length-based routing is very important for Tenstorrent. However, we can address this later once the technique becomes more concrete.
